speaker: interviewee
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four, an interview with Robert Ness, on August the 24th, 1995. So after you passed the test, then, of the thousand-- saying the thousand in the right way--

speaker: interviewee
text: So they let me stay and sleep overnight because it was already curfew time. And they figured that they would let me go in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in the guard house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the guard house, on the floor. I was sleeping right there. One of-- one of them came up to me, put him against the wall. But you know, I understood whatever they're saying, you know? I was playing I don't know German. But I knew what they were saying and what goes on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as a matter of fact, one of them was working on-- on the crossword puzzles. So I helped him with the geography part and Africa and so forth. But I kept it low. And-- and I figured, OK. Morning is up. I was ready to let me go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, one of them comes. Says, [NON-ENGLISH] says a ghetto tour-- it's a ghetto gate. And he wants a youngster to be brought to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, [NON-ENGLISH] was the German head--

speaker: interviewee
text: --of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: --of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he used to-- just about-- regularly, without 10, 15 killings was nothing for him. That was spit. It was just-- was an appetizer for dinner or lunch. And as they come in, there was this gate. And next to the gate was this post, kind of like a point where you cross individually, and the gate for opening up for bigger stuff. And he immediately took out the gun, put it to my head-- [NON-ENGLISH] speak German. And-- and I said again, [NON-ENGLISH]. And he-- and he started again. And then there's some back and forth and so on. Take off the pants. Drop the pants. Believe it or not, the mohel-- the-- the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Circumcisor.

speaker: interviewee
text: The circumcisor who did it-- he didn't do a very good job on me. Because there is a little piece-- the top is not fully cut. Do you follow me? In other words, the circumcision is just about-- maybe let's say, 7/8. Somehow or other, I took with my hand the lower part. And I kind of moved it around so. And he said, [NON-ENGLISH]-- no, not circumcised. But then he looked closer. And I mentioned to you, the ghetto used to become closer and closer. They used to liquidate. So it was-- the end result was about six ghettos within the ghetto. So people used to go and help themself some clothes and I did, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I helped myself to a little winter coat-- a very nice one-- a beautiful one-- warm one. And as careful as I was-- and I was careful about these darn stars, you know? Because it gets shades after a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't notice it. And suddenly he looks. He looks closer and closer. And he says, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: You're a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Out. Because he saw the shadow of the Star of David on the-- on the-- on the left part of the-- of the coat. [NON-ENGLISH] And he gets me. That's where he used to kill the people. I figured that was it. Suddenly, before he just ended, the police chief comes-- the Jewish policeman. He comes in and he starts talking to me in Yiddish, but slowly and distinctly that any German could understand it. It was-- [NON-ENGLISH] It's-- any German would understand a [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: That you're a Jew. Tell him you're a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: He won't do anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: He will do-- that's right. [NON-ENGLISH] He will do nothing to you. [NON-ENGLISH] And then he says that my-- that your father and sister went to ask me to come and talk to you. And just tell him that's the-- [NON-ENGLISH] So [NON-ENGLISH] I said, yeah. Hah. He gave me a kick in the-- in the behind. And then, suddenly, the-- the guards wanted to get somebody to clean their place and the place where I stayed overnight. And he was coming there-- when he kicked me, he said, [NON-ENGLISH]. I recognize a Jew at any time. Nobody passes me. And most probably, that's the reason why he sent me back to the guard place to go and clean for them. It was probably to show off that, you see, you guys-- bunch of jerks-- couldn't-- little Jewish boy. You couldn't figure out that he's a Jew. But I did. I mean, I think that that was the reason. I'm not saying it was the reasoning at that time. Later on, after the war, I kind of saw that, most probably, was the reason.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went back there. Well, oh, you were lying to us, weren't you? Huh? You don't speak German? Huh? And this. And I should get 10 [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, 10-- Blows.

speaker: interviewee
text: Blows. I had to take off my pants and lay down over the table. I didn't get 10. I got about 5. And I had-- they let me have my hands to catch. But I was lying to them. I said, well, you know, I just did say it's-- yeah, you're going to work. Ah, it's easy to work. You take everybody and you probably kill us all. And I really was fed up. I didn't care what they do at that point. And they throw me back into the ghetto. And the very next day, I was-- after I told him the story and what, as tired and exhausted as I was, the very next day during daytime, there was a guard walking around. We opened out a little hall. And then my father and I ran out to the main synagogue. We stayed there till it became dark at night. And then we moved from one ghetto to the other. You know, you had to pass when you hear some noises. And he would came out to the-- to the last gates. We crossed that, moved up. And then we went to the second ghetto, which was closed. And nobody was there. It was empty. We stayed overnight.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was cold. I'm talking now beginning of-- end of February, March, 1943. OK? It was cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stayed there. Then we went to Tadek's place. He wasn't there. So it was-- his brother-in-law was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said he would take us. My father didn't like that. And we came. And then we met Tadek next day and he took us to Vilna. But that was quite a ride in itself. That's another story. Just before we leave Grodno, you had a bar mitzvah in Grodno.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Absolutely. Can you imagine a bar mitzvah in the ghetto? And that was in October of 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was a synagogue. And there was a service. And I-- you know, as I told you, I didn't have to learn how to sing the [NON-ENGLISH] or how to sing the [NON-ENGLISH] that teach children sing for thereafter. I knew it because of the fact I had my daily lesson-- weekly lessons for years. So it was nothing to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Germans allowed a service.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, obviously, there was still a synagogue. But you see that what it was, that we left from Slonim. We heard that in the Reich, it's kind of different. It's easier. And when we came to Grodno, we couldn't believe our eyes. There was a synagogue. They had-- it was just a completely different life. Much easier. You could buy newspapers. I used to go crazy to get newspapers. I could go buy them in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember about the service itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember what [NON-ENGLISH] it was. I know I had my bar mitzvah. I was called to the Torah. My father was there. People were crying on the conditions that here, a man-- a boy, has a bar mitzvah. And I had my bar mitzvah. I read my [NON-ENGLISH]. And I read my section from the-- from the scriptures, whatever it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall at that time what your religious feelings were? Did you still have any kind of religious belief?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, you know. I am mad with God for Auschwitz, no question about it. I always said, where was he? But at that time, no, I didn't. I didn't have this feeling yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was Tadek. And his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soroka, S-O--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tadek--

speaker: interviewee
text: Tadeusz.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was Tadeusz.

speaker: interviewee
text: T-A-D-E-U-S-Z. Soroka, S-O-R-O-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he brought you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took us to--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you and your father and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just-- no. Me and my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just you and your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went out of the ghetto the next day. Leah and Aaron were still left there. And he took me and my father. And that is-- that in itself-- a story, you know? It's-- it was in a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the picture, we can show later. The railway car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the railway car. And he took us in this railway car.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, we can--

speaker: interviewee
text: You can show it later. They can--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. We'll show it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He took us in this railway car, which had a little vestibule, as you can see. We jumped on the-- on the car. And we took us and we came to a place on the border between Lithuania and Germany. That's this new border, Lithuania-Germany. And we stayed in the car for awhile. And then we were out of the car. And then Tadek-- we waited for train to go. And that-- we waited and waited. Tadek said, no. That train is not going. So we-- we went to-- to a car like this-- like this one. I don't remember whether the car-- maybe it was a similar car that we went from Grodno to the border. But then we went-- we went to this car in this vestibule. And all three of us were there. And this train was supposed to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we sit there and sit there. And suddenly, a German railroad policeman starts knocking on the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Everybody quiet. He's persistent, knocks again. Obviously, Tadek saw that this is-- he has to do something about it. So he said, yah, yah. And he went out. Instead of going out-- the soldier was on the outside. It has two doors.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he went from the other door and moved around. And I never forgot that. When they spot him, he ask him-- [NON-ENGLISH] Is anybody there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Is somebody else there? In German. And Tadek said, nein, nein. If he would have insisted, it would have been the end of Tadek and father and me-- all three of us. He didn't and he took Tadek with him to the station. And we waited. And those hour and a half or two was just like-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: ages-- ages. People get gray from a two hours like that sitting in this cubby hole and waiting. Tadek is coming? Was he arrested? Did he-- is this the end of him? What are we going to do? And lo and behold, Tadek shows up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, this train is going to stay here for three or four days. And he says, we have to move to another train. And he took us out from this caboose, or this cubby hole-- this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Little vestibule.

speaker: interviewee
text: Vestibule, right. He took us out of there. And we're going on a German military train-- Red Cross. So we get on the train. And Tadek said, we have to go on the roof. Mind you, I'm talking now, this is end of February, beginning of March. It's cold. It's slippery. And the roof is not a straight, flat roof. It's like that, an oval shape. And my father said, I'm not going. I said, absolutely. I'm not going on top of that roof. So Tadek was on the roof. And I was just about ready to go. But he could see that. Then he says, I'm not going.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you know, imagine that they are-- those plates that hit when one car comes to the other, they hit one another. And then there are arms-- their arms don't move in. Their arms are steady, except the plates move back and forth. So Tadek was on the roof. Then he went down. My father sat on one side of a plate and I was in front of the plate. In other words, when-- if a train was coming from the other side, I was getting there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the train almost pulled me off from my seat. I was sitting on that arm when that second train coming in big speed, you know, and this one going in big speed. It almost pulled me off. I just barely caught the-- the-- the handle there to hold on to-- to stay on. And then it became morning. And some German officers came out. And oh, they just talked as if nothing. Probably they are used to this idea of civilian-- mind you, they don't know we are Jews-- a civilian riding from one city to another. There are no trains going so you hitchhike a ride. And that's how we came in this military train to Vilna. We passed Ponary. We knew about Ponary-- already heard about it. Daddy was black from the-- from the smoke and so on. And he looked and-- and Tadek brought us into the city. And from there, we got-- he brought us close to the ghetto and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you entered the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we entered the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anybody in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But when we entered the ghetto, it was an interesting story in itself, again. There was-- when we entered the ghetto, there was a fellow by the name of Desler. And he was a Jewish policeman. Believe it or not, there is a play-- where he is a main character-- about the-- the ghetto Vilna. Because there is some connection with us, too, again. And he started. We told him that we were in Slonim and we run away from Grodno and now we are in Vilna.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said to my father, [NON-ENGLISH]. Don't spread the stories. [NON-ENGLISH] Don't create any stuff. Keep quiet about it. You mean about the--

speaker: interviewee
text: About the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The slaughters.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. The slaughters and the so forth. Not that it was any news for Vilna that they were-- they had their own slaughters. But they were already quiet for a while. So they figured maybe that's the end of it. Now we are already-- not to create. But anyway-- and that's how we got into the ghetto. And then about-- must have been two weeks later, Leah and Aaron showed up, and another-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- a third one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tadek had brought them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tadek brought them, too. They got out of the ghetto. By that time, it was only two, three houses left from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: From--

speaker: interviewee
text: From the whole Grodno ghetto. There was only two, three houses left. When we left, there were already about five, six houses. When they went, it was already a few houses. And he took them. They have their own ride story-- quite a story. And they came. And the thing looked like, suddenly, the liquidation of the Vilna ghetto is beginning to take place. I'm talking now summer, 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in the Vilna ghetto then?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Vilna ghetto, we lived on Strashun, 8. It was a big kind of place with a yard and we managed to live there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: How? There was-- you know, in a room like that, would have been, maybe, three families living, you know? It was-- it was packed-- sardines.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had-- we still had it pretty good. A smaller room where Leah, Aaron-- I don't know whether Aaron lived with us. I think he did. Aaron and my father and I.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they went to-- my father worked, supposedly, in the electrical station. So he used to go outside of the ghetto. That's where Aaron worked and his friend-- the other one, Lipshits.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't? You were not working?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't-- I used to prepare for them. I was a big cook.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was getting horse meat. There were-- they had even cards. Whoever heard? Jews getting ration cards in Grodno-- in, Vilna too, you know? You still got something there. And-- Did you hear at all about the Warsaw ghetto rebellion?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. I don't remember too much details of it. But I knew that it was supposedly that people stood up in Warsaw and there was an uprising-- something. Not too much detail, not too much. I didn't hear it in-- I wouldn't have heard it in Grodno because it was in-- in May.

speaker: interviewer
text: It would have been in Vilna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Vilna. I don't think so. I don't remember having heard it. But there was an underground, I know. Leah and my brother-in-law were-- were in the underground. And they left. Leah and Aaron left and in the woods to the partisans. I maybe could have, perhaps, gone with them. I don't know. It wasn't quite-- but be that as it may, I remember having talked to my father. And I-- and I said-- I used to call him papa. I said, Papa, whatever happens with you, we-- I am going to stay with you whatever it is. I think Father never forgot that. You know, here is-- everybody is kind of for himself. Life is cheap. I said-- I remember. I was still a boy-- 13, not 14 yet-- going on 14. I said that, you know, you lost your wife-- my mother and sister and whatever it is. And I went through with him so much. I went through with him in Slonim, the prison, the separation, and all the other stuff. But anyhow, so he made some connection to go outside the ghetto in a hiding place. And-- and a farmer by the name of Kozlowski in a village called [NON-ENGLISH]. And my father and I go out of the ghetto and we move and we go.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I'm talking July or August-- end of July, beginning of August-- maybe middle of August-- 1943. Not-- not the-- not middle of August. It's end of July, beginning of August.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we pass a village. And who do we have to bump in but some woman who was kind of a nosey one. She was married to a policeman-- a Lithuanian policeman. She started asking questions. Where are you going? and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we come to this village of [NON-ENGLISH]. And we come to Kozlowski. And my father is outside in the woods. I didn't know where he was. Later I found out he was in the doghouse. He hid in the yard. And I am in the kitchen with all the other-- with the visitors they have-- Poles-- like a Polish boy. My Polish was good. My looks were-- still had my little [NON-ENGLISH]-- this gymnasium hat, you know, that's-- that you see on the picture there. And-- and suddenly, a shot is fired. I hear a few shots are fired. The police was in, they were talking, looking around. And they run out. And then we hear some noises. And then the woman-- the woman, she says, no, no. You cannot stay here. And absolutely not.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went into the field of poppy-- poppy seed field and I stayed there overnight. And I knew the influence of poppy seeds-- just fell asleep. Slept through like a-- like, really-- would you say like a baby slept.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remembered it was Sunday. And I woke up. I said, what am I going-- she doesn't-- I cannot go back here. I don't know where my father is. So I figure I go back to the ghetto. But on the way to the ghetto, I had to pass that village again where this darn woman was. And she was saying that the police came to look for Jews. They said there was a grandfather and a grandchild. You see, my father with gray hair. And he was wearing what you'd call a [NON-ENGLISH]-- a type of a farmer type of hat that was worn in Poland. And-- and I start walking. And there is a guard in the road. And as I passed him, he calls me back. He calls me back. And I don't know where I had the nerve. But I-- I look at him. And I say, oh, [NON-ENGLISH] Oh, good morning. I see an acquaintance is calling. And I push my five fingers toward him. And he kind of slowly reaches out with his hand. And he said, [NON-ENGLISH] It kind of doesn't look like an acquaintance here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, well, weren't you at Kozlowski's place last night and looking for the Jews? He said, yes. Did you catch any? He said, yeah, four of them. I'm guarding them. We became pals already. He-- he said, what are you-- what are you doing? Where are you going? I said, well, I'm going to church. I'm going to Vilna to church. It's Sunday morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [NON-ENGLISH] Goodbye, goodbye. And I walked away just like that-- just like that. I don't know where I had this idea to come up. I just-- be that as it may, that's how I started. Never forgot that. [NON-ENGLISH] Well, I see an acquaintance is calling.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I started to go back to the city and I went back to the ghetto. I went back to the ghetto and then I found out-- I was still a few weeks in the ghetto. I found out that father was alive and that he is there in someplace around Kozlowski. And suddenly the ghetto is closed, September the 1st. Liquidated? Liquidation--

speaker: interviewee
text: Liquidation starts taking the people. They didn't quite liquidate the whole ghetto. But they took about 6,000 people to Estonia. And I was among them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go to Estonia, there were some--

speaker: interviewee
text: There is also some interesting incidents there. I didn't go yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were some songs that you told me about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. But I just-- let me-- just before going to Estonia-- because it's very interesting. Mind you, here I'm 13, 14 years old. I'm not 14 yet-- almost 14. We come to the train and they lead us for Estonia. I didn't believe it. I saw this. That's it. I mean, run, run, run. That's the end of the ballgame. That's the end of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Desler shows up. You remember I told you about the policeman, Desler, when we entered the ghetto? A Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish policeman. And I see him. I say, Desler, Desler. He came up to me. And he says-- I said to him, do me a favor. Give me a piece of paper. I write a note to my father. I think my father is alive. When-- in case he comes back to the ghetto, give it to him. And I wrote down and I never forgot it. Here I think-- that's why I think that revenge, in my case, played such a big role in survival. I write to my daddy-- to my father-- thinking-- and I write to him. I say, look. I was running away. I can't anymore. This is the end. Take revenge. [NON-ENGLISH] Take [NON-ENGLISH] for the innocent blood of Mother, Pola, and me. And I signed my name. [SOBBING] Believe it or not, then my father got it from him when he came back to the ghetto. When he left the ghetto to join Leah in the woods, he had it with him. He used to show off with it to everybody. He wanted to see about his little boy-- what he wrote before his death, so to speak. And I saw it after the war. He carried it with him in a postal card-- like a Russian postal card-- tied up. And you know, we had it. And I don't know what happened to it-- misplaced. What a document that was.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I-- I do remember the content. It was written with an [INAUDIBLE] type of pencil, you know? And it-- it was written in Yiddish. So father kind of felt like that was the end. But then-- and that-- that transport left for Estonia. You want to go on further? There's still some time there? Sure. Just maybe-- if you feel up to it, the songs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK. Yeah. There was a few songs. I will write it out for you. I don't have it written out. But one of them was-- you know, I'm not talking about the general songs in the ghetto. It's well known and people know them and so forth. But there were some songs. One of them was from the ghetto of Grodno about a Jewish policeman-- that he dreams about being at the gate. The reason there's a gate-- because there is where the action is. That's where you can smuggle in some stuff and get a part of it if you work with somebody bringing in food from outside the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he says that he is-- he is watching the cemetery. The cemetery was right smack in the ghetto. And so young couples-- where could they go in any place? So they used to go to the cemetery. That used to be their love nest to meet somebody. So I said-- so he says, I am watching the cemetery and-- where the young people don't give rest to the deceased. And I can only dream about the ghetto gates. And my shoes-- I'm pretty wearing out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all I want is five mark and a loaf of bread. Now this, I remember. Now, it's in Polish, but I gave you the content of it. And it goes like this. [SINGING IN POLISH] Everybody's dreaming about it. [SINGING IN POLISH] This is the cemetery part. [SINGING IN POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that is from the ghetto of Grodno, which is-- Which is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which is-- nobody heard it. Nobody knows it. It's just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. This is just-- I'm positive that I never heard it from anybody. I was-- I liked songs. I used to catch them on it. And that's part of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
